title: Fermentation Traditions Around the World
subtitle: A journey through the cultures, vessels, and techniques that preserve food across every continent

image.caption: Kimjang - the Korean communal kimchi-making tradition, a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage

sections.content: Fermentation is humanity's oldest biotechnology [cite:c1]. Long before anyone understood bacteria, pH, or metabolic pathways, communities on every continent independently discovered that salt, time, and the right conditions could transform perishable food into something that lasted months or years - and tasted extraordinary in the process. This article surveys the major fermentation traditions of the world, with special attention to the terminology, vessels, and cultural practices that make each tradition unique.

No country on Earth is more deeply identified with fermentation than Korea. Korean cuisine features fermented foods at virtually every meal, and the country has developed sophisticated fermentation traditions over thousands of years [cite:c2]. The Korean term banchan (side dishes) is dominated by fermented preparations, and the Korean pantry revolves around fermented staples.

Kimjang is the traditional Korean communal practice of making large quantities of kimchi in late autumn, typically November, to sustain families through the winter. Entire neighbourhoods and extended families gather to prepare hundreds of kilograms of cabbage, radish, and other vegetables, sharing the labour of salting, seasoning, and packing. In 2013, UNESCO inscribed Kimjang on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, recognising it as 'a significant practice that affirms Korean identity and strengthens family cooperation' [cite:c3]. Modern Kimjang still takes place across Korea, though many urban families now make smaller quantities.

Onggi are traditional Korean earthenware pots used for fermenting and storing food. What makes onggi remarkable is their breathability - the porous, unglazed clay allows microscopic amounts of air to circulate, creating an environment that's ideal for slow, controlled fermentation. The clay's mineral composition may also contribute beneficial trace elements. Traditional Korean households kept rows of onggi on a raised platform called a jangdokdae, typically on the sunniest part of the property, where doenjang, gochujang, ganjang (soy sauce), and kimchi aged for months or years.

Korean red pepper flakes, an essential ingredient in kimchi. Unlike other chilli flakes, gochugaru is made from sun-dried red peppers that are deseeded and coarsely ground, producing a vibrant red powder with a smoky, slightly sweet heat. The flavour is distinctly different from cayenne, paprika, or Indian chilli powder. Gochugaru provides both the signature colour and a layer of gentle warmth to kimchi, kkakdugi [[recipe:kkakdugi-radish]], and other Korean ferments.

Korean fermented soybean paste, analogous to but distinct from Japanese miso. Doenjang is traditionally made from meju - blocks of cooked, mashed soybeans that are tied with rice straw and hung to ferment for weeks, developing a complex mould culture. The meju is then broken into brine and fermented for months in onggi. The resulting paste has a deeper, more pungent flavour than most Japanese miso varieties.

Salted and fermented seafood, a cornerstone of Korean cuisine. The most common varieties are saeujeot (fermented shrimp) and myeolchi-jeot (fermented anchovies), which are essential umami components in traditional kimchi recipes. Jeotgal provides both saltiness and a deep, savory complexity that salt alone cannot achieve. The fermentation of fish proteins by LAB and enzymes produces glutamate and other flavour compounds responsible for the characteristic 'fifth taste.'

Japanese fermentation is characterised by meticulous technique, long ageing periods, and a deep respect for the microorganisms involved [cite:c4]. Many Japanese fermented foods rely on koji (Aspergillus oryzae), a filamentous mould that is cultivated with extraordinary care.

Aspergillus oryzae, cultivated on steamed rice, barley, or soybeans. Koji is arguably the most important microorganism in East Asian cuisine - it is the foundation of miso, soy sauce (shoyu), sake, mirin, rice vinegar, and shio koji. The mould produces powerful enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases) that break down starches into sugars and proteins into amino acids, enabling subsequent fermentation by yeasts and bacteria. In Japan, koji cultivation is such a specialised skill that koji makers (tane-koji-ya) have operated as dedicated businesses for centuries, some for over a thousand years.

Fermented soybean paste made by combining cooked soybeans with koji and salt, then ageing for weeks to years. Varieties range from shiro miso (white, sweet, aged 1–3 months) to hatcho miso (dark, intensely savoury, aged 1–3 years). The colour and flavour depend on the koji substrate (rice, barley, or soybean), the proportion of soybeans, salt content, and ageing time. Miso is consumed daily in Japan, primarily as miso soup (misoshiru), and is one of the most concentrated sources of umami in any cuisine.

The umbrella term for all Japanese preserved vegetables. Tsukemono encompasses a vast range of techniques including shiozuke (salt pickling - closest to Western lacto-fermentation), kasuzuke (sake lees pickling), misozuke (miso pickling), and suzuke (vinegar pickling). The most common tsukemono are quick salt pickles served as accompaniments to rice, but some varieties are aged for months.

A fascinating fermentation technique unique to Japan, where vegetables are buried in a bed of rice bran (nuka) mixed with salt, water, konbu, and chilli. The nuka bed is a living ecosystem of LAB and yeasts that must be stirred by hand daily to prevent off-flavours. A well-maintained nuka bed can be passed down through generations - some families claim beds that are over 100 years old. The resulting pickles have a complex, umami-rich flavour unlike any other fermentation method.

A versatile seasoning made by fermenting koji rice with salt and water for 7–14 days. Shio koji functions as a marinade, tenderiser, and umami booster all in one. The enzymes from the koji break down proteins in meat and fish, making them more tender, while generating glutamate and other savoury compounds. It has become increasingly popular outside Japan as a 'secret weapon' ingredient.
South Asian fermentation traditions are deeply integrated with the subcontinent's rich spice culture, monsoon climate, and the practical need to preserve food through hot, humid seasons [cite:c5].

The pan-South Asian tradition of pickling, found across India, Pakistan, Nepal, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. Unlike Western fermentation which relies primarily on salt and LAB, achaar techniques typically combine salt with oil (mustard oil in the north, sesame oil in the south), spices (fenugreek, mustard seeds, asafoetida, turmeric), and often sun-drying. The result is a complex preservation system where oil creates an anaerobic barrier, salt and spices provide antimicrobial effects, and LAB contribute sourness over time. Every Indian household has its own achaar tradition, often with recipes passed through generations and fiercely guarded family secrets. Mango achaar, lime achaar, and mixed vegetable achaar are staples across the subcontinent.

Indian yogurt, one of the most widely consumed fermented foods in the world [[recipe:dahi-homemade]]. Unlike commercial Western yogurt which uses specific starter cultures, traditional dahi is made by adding a spoonful of previous dahi to warm milk - a backslopping technique that maintains a complex, evolving microbial community unique to each household. Dahi is central to Indian cuisine: eaten plain, as raita, in lassi and chaas, and as a marinade for meats and vegetables.

A fermented beverage from North India made with black carrots (or beetroot), mustard seeds, and salt water [[recipe:kanji-rice]]. The deep purple drink is naturally probiotic and is traditionally consumed during Holi and through the summer months as a cooling, digestive tonic. The mustard seeds provide antimicrobial compounds that help select for LAB while adding a distinctive pungency.

European fermentation traditions were driven by the need to preserve the autumn harvest through long, cold winters [cite:c1]. Cool cellars provided natural temperature control, allowing slow fermentations that developed complex flavours.

The defining fermented food of Central Europe, sauerkraut is finely shredded cabbage fermented with salt - nothing more [[recipe:sauerkraut-classic]]. The tradition runs deep in Germany, Austria, Poland, Alsace (France), and the Czech Republic, where families historically made it in large wooden barrels or stoneware crocks in autumn, stamping the cabbage with their feet to extract juice. It was the survival vegetable - providing vitamin C through winters when fresh produce was unavailable. German and Eastern European immigrants brought sauerkraut traditions to the Americas, where it became a staple of deli cuisine.

The lightly fermented cabbage relish of El Salvador [[recipe:curtido-salvadoran]], served alongside pupusas (stuffed corn cakes) as a standard accompaniment. Curtido is similar to sauerkraut but is distinguished by the addition of onion, carrot, oregano, and sometimes red pepper flakes. It's typically fermented for just 1–3 days at warm Central American temperatures, resulting in a crunchy, mildly sour condiment that balances the richness of fried foods.

Turkish pickled vegetables, a vibrant and essential part of Turkish cuisine. Tursu encompasses a wide range of pickled vegetables - peppers, cucumbers, cabbage, green tomatoes, carrots, and garlic - typically preserved in a brine of salt, vinegar, and sometimes citric acid. Turkish families traditionally make tursu in large glass jars visible in markets, and specialised tursu shops (turşucu) sell dozens of varieties. The juice (tursu suyu) is consumed as a beverage, valued as a digestive and hangover remedy.

Fermentation driven by lactic acid bacteria (Lactobacillus and related genera) [[article:lactic-acid-bacteria]], which convert sugars to lactic acid [[article:how-lacto-fermentation-works]]. The foundation of most vegetable ferments worldwide. The name derives from the Latin lac (milk) because these bacteria were first isolated from soured milk - not because they require lactose.

A solution of salt dissolved in water, used to submerge vegetables for fermentation [[article:salt-and-fermentation]]. Brine concentrations typically range from 2% (light, for quick ferments) to 10% (heavy, for long preservation). The brine serves multiple purposes: it maintains anaerobic conditions by keeping food submerged, provides the salt concentration needed for selective microbial pressure, and becomes a flavourful liquid that can be used as a starter for new ferments or consumed directly.

A thin film that forms on the surface of some fermenting liquids. In kombucha, the pellicle is the visible part of the SCOBY - a mat of cellulose produced by bacteria. In vegetable ferments, a surface pellicle is usually kahm yeast [[article:troubleshooting-ferments]], which is harmless but may affect flavour if left for extended periods.

A collective name for several species of aerobic film-forming yeasts (Pichia, Candida, Hansenula) that can appear as a white, wrinkled film on the surface of ferments [[article:troubleshooting-ferments]]. Kahm yeast is not harmful and is not mould - it's a cosmetic issue. It can be skimmed off and the ferment continues normally. Prevention: keep vegetables submerged and minimise oxygen exposure.

Symbiotic Culture of Bacteria and Yeast - the living culture used to brew kombucha. The SCOBY appears as a rubbery, pancake-like mat of cellulose that floats on the surface of sweetened tea. It houses a complex community of acetic acid bacteria (Komagataeibacter) and yeasts (Brettanomyces, Zygosaccharomyces) that work together to convert sweet tea into the tangy, slightly effervescent fermented beverage.

A preparation of beneficial microorganisms used to inoculate a new ferment. Common starter cultures include: a spoonful of previous dahi for new dahi [[recipe:dahi-homemade]], brine from an active ferment ('backslopping'), whey strained from yogurt, or commercially produced freeze-dried cultures. Most vegetable ferments don't require a starter - the LAB are already present on the vegetables [[article:lactic-acid-bacteria]]. Starters are mainly used in dairy ferments and in situations where you want to ensure a specific flavour profile.
